title: The Neuroscience of Awe and Why Psychedelics Reliably Produce It
body:
The Neuroscience of Awe and Why Psychedelics Reliably Produce It

1. Opening hook - personal anecdote or provocative question

I still remember the first time I felt real awe under the influence of psilocybin mushrooms. It wasn’t just a visual spectacle or a flood of emotions. It was as if the world had cracked open and revealed something vast, ancient, and deeply meaningful. The sky wasn’t just blue anymore. It was alive. The trees whispered secrets I'd never heard before. And at the core of that experience was a feeling I can only describe as awe - wild, humbling, and utterly life-changing.

Awe is this strange emotion that seems to catch you off guard. You’re walking along, your mind buzzing with everyday worries, and suddenly, you stop in your tracks. Maybe it’s a sunset that blazes across the horizon, a towering mountain, or a moment of unexpected kindness. A rush of something bigger than you wells up inside. Time feels different. You feel connected to something outside yourself, and your usual sense of importance shrinks.

But why is awe so compelling? Why does it leave us breathless and changed? And why do psychedelics, especially mushrooms, so reliably spark this feeling? These questions have been circling my mind for years - as a therapist, a researcher, and someone who's sat quietly with the medicine through countless sessions. Awe is not just a nice side effect. It’s a fundamental shift in consciousness that psychedelics dial up with remarkable precision. Understanding what’s going on in the brain during these moments can reveal a lot about why these medicines hold such power.

I’m not alone in this fascination. Scientists like Roland Griffiths at Johns Hopkins, Robin Carhart-Harris at Imperial College, and ethnobotanists like Paul Stamets have all contributed pieces of the puzzle. They've helped us see awe not just as poetic fluff but as a measurable, brain-based phenomenon intimately linked to mystical experiences. These mystical experiences - involving awe, interconnectedness, and transcendence - are often predictors of lasting positive change after psychedelic therapy.

So, what exactly happens in our brains when awe washes over us? How do psychedelics tap into this ancient emotional circuitry? And what can we, as mushroom enthusiasts, therapists, or seekers, learn from this? Let’s unpack these questions, drawing from neuroscience, psychology, and firsthand experience. Because awe, as I’ve come to know it through my own journeys and those shared with clients, isn’t just a fleeting high. It’s a door to a deeper understanding of life, healing, and the self.

2. Core concept explanation with scientific backing

Awe is a complex emotional state that neuroscientists are only beginning to map out in the brain. At its core, awe arises when we encounter something vast that challenges our usual mental structure or worldview. This “vastness” can be physical - like a towering canyon - or conceptual - like contemplating the universe’s infinity. The experience forces an accommodation in our mental schemas, a cognitive recalibration to make room for something bigger.

Neuroscientifically, awe involves a distinct pattern of brain activity. Research shows that areas like the prefrontal cortex, especially the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC), the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), and the default mode network (DMN) are key players. The DMN, in particular, is a network of brain regions active during self-referential thought, mind-wandering, and autobiographical memory. When awe hits, this network quiets down, reducing the emphasis on self-centered thinking. You feel less “me” and more part of a vast “we.”

This DMN suppression is a hallmark of classic psychedelic action. Compounds like psilocybin disrupt the usual connectivity within the DMN, temporarily fracturing the ego’s tight hold on consciousness. That’s why feelings of ego dissolution, or loss of self-boundaries, are common. And as the DMN dims, other brain areas involving sensory processing and emotional salience light up. This sensory flooding combined with reduced self-focus creates the perfect storm for awe.

Additionally, psychedelics stimulate serotonin 2A receptors, which are densely located in cortical areas responsible for higher cognition and perception. This receptor activation increases cortical entropy - essentially introducing more randomness and flexibility into brain activity. This makes perception and cognition more fluid, allowing for novel connections and interpretations, which can manifest as awe-inspiring visions, feelings of interconnectedness, or mystical experiences.

Psychologist Jonathan Haidt describes awe as “an emotional response to perceptually vast stimuli that transcend current frames of reference.” Neuroscientist Dacher Keltner calls it a “self-transcendent emotion” that expands our sense of time and space. This expansion and reframing, triggered by psychedelics, are what make awe such a deep and reliable effect in these medicines.

Interestingly, not all awe experiences are equal. Neuroscientific studies indicate two types: positive awe, which uplifts and connects us, and threat-based awe, which can induce fear or anxiety when confronted with overwhelming natural forces. Psychedelic awe most often falls in the positive category, especially when set and setting are carefully managed.

To conclude this section, awe isn’t just some poetic word for “I was amazed.” It’s a deeply wired emotion involving complex brain dynamics, serotonin receptor activity, and a temporary loosening of ego boundaries. Psychedelics tap into these mechanisms with a precision that no other experience quite matches, reliably producing this powerful feeling that can change how you see yourself and the world.

3. Research and evidence - cite specific studies by name

Over the last two decades, research on psychedelics and awe has shifted from fringe curiosity to rigorous science. One of the landmark studies on this subject was led by Roland Griffiths and his team at Johns Hopkins University. Their 2006 study, published in Psychopharmacology, administered psilocybin to healthy volunteers in controlled settings. They found that psilocybin reliably induced mystical-type experiences characterized by awe, a sense of unity, sacredness, and ineffability. These experiences correlated strongly with long-term positive changes in mood and behavior.

Further research expanded on this. In a 2011 follow-up study, Griffiths showed that these awe-inspiring mystical experiences predicted sustained decreases in depression and anxiety, especially in patients battling life-threatening illnesses. This was a breakthrough because it linked awe experiences under psychedelics to measurable clinical outcomes, moving beyond anecdote.

On the neuroscience side, the work of Robin Carhart-Harris at Imperial College London added another layer of understanding. In his 2012 brain imaging study using fMRI, Carhart-Harris demonstrated that psilocybin reduced activity and connectivity in the default mode network - the brain’s self-referential hub. This correlated with subjective reports of ego dissolution and awe. His follow-up studies in 2016 and beyond also showed increased brain network flexibility and entropy, which likely underpin the novel perceptions and emotional openness that awe requires.

Ethnobotanist Paul Stamets, meanwhile, has been instrumental in highlighting the cooperation between natural compounds in mushrooms and their effects on brain plasticity. While he focuses largely on Lion’s Mane for neurogenesis, which you can learn more about through Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules, his insights connect the dots on how psychedelics not only trigger awe but can also help the brain grow and heal afterward.

Another key player is Stanislav Grof, one of the pioneers of psychedelic therapy. His decades of clinical work describe awe as a doorway to what he calls “transpersonal experiences,” where the usual boundaries between self and other dissolve. Grof’s phenomenological work, although predating modern brain imaging, laid the groundwork for understanding awe as both a psychological and spiritual turning point.

emotion research, Dacher Keltner’s studies on awe emphasize its social and evolutionary functions. His work suggests awe motivates prosocial behavior and expands cognitive flexibility, which complements the therapeutic effects seen in psychedelics.

For those wanting a more narrative take on this science, Michael Pollan’s book How to Change Your Mind beautifully weaves these studies and personal stories together, making the science accessible without sacrificing nuance.

All these studies converge on one point: psychedelics reliably produce awe by disrupting normal brain networks, opening up new ways of perceiving and connecting. This awe is not just a fleeting emotion but a catalyst for lasting change.

4. Practical application - how readers can apply this

Understanding the neuroscience of awe and psychedelic effects sets the stage for more intentional use of these medicines. If awe is a key ingredient in the therapeutic and life-changing properties of psilocybin, how can we encourage it in our own experiences - whether professionally or personally?

First, set and setting remain essential. Awe flourishes in environments that feel safe, inspiring, and connected to nature or art. I always suggest spending time outside before or after a session - whether it’s a walk in the woods or sitting beneath a starry sky. These natural stimuli naturally prime the brain for awe. The brain’s visual and sensory systems engage with stimuli that feel bigger than ourselves, nudging us toward that feeling.

Next, prepare your mindset. Approach the experience with curiosity rather than expectation or control. Psychedelic awe arises from encountering the unknown in a way that challenges your worldview. Rigid expectations can block this. Instead, try journaling or meditating on what “vastness” or “connection” means to you before dosing. This primes your cognitive schema for accommodation.

Integration practices are just as important. After the session, spend time reflecting on moments of awe and their emotional impact. This might involve talking with a trusted guide or therapist who understands psychedelic medicine or using journals and art to process your experience. Books like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk provide excellent insights into how the body and mind store and release these emotional shifts.

Finally, consider combining your psychedelic journey with supportive supplements like Lion’s Mane, which promotes neuroplasticity. While Lion’s Mane is not psychedelic, its ability to stimulate brain growth can complement the rewiring initiated by psilocybin. You can find quality capsules from trusted sources like Host Defense.

For therapists and facilitators, understanding the neuroscience behind awe can improve session design. Encouraging moments of silence, nature immersion, or engaging with art can help clients access awe more readily. Training in trauma-informed care also ensures that awe doesn’t tip into overwhelm or fear.

In short, awe isn’t just spontaneous magic. It can be nurtured by creating the right mental, physical, and emotional conditions before, during, and after a psychedelic experience. Paying attention to these details helps the brain stay open and receptive to the awe that leads to healing and insight.

5. Common challenges and how to work through them

While awe can be uplifting and healing, it doesn’t always come easy or feel purely pleasant. One common challenge is the overwhelming nature of awe itself. The vastness and loss of normal boundaries can trigger fear, anxiety, or confusion. In these moments, the same brain mechanisms that allow for awe - DMN downregulation and ego dissolution - can feel like losing control instead of gaining insight.

Many first-timers report this as a “bad trip” or a difficult experience. But these challenging moments often contain deep lessons if approached with care. The key is grounding. Techniques like deep breathing, body scanning, or focusing on a trusted object can anchor you back to the present, helping the brain re-establish safety signals. This is one reason integration therapy that includes somatic work, like the approaches taught by Bessel van der Kolk in The Body Keeps the Score, are so valuable after psychedelic sessions.

Another difficulty is the fleeting nature of awe. Sometimes, you feel awe in the moment but struggle to hold onto it afterward. The brain is wired to revert to familiar patterns, so the expanded perspectives from psychedelics can quickly fade. This is where integration becomes essential. Daily practices like mindfulness, spending time in nature, or creative expression can help keep the awe-inspired mindset alive.

There’s also the challenge of navigating awe in the context of trauma or mental health issues. For some, awe might trigger memories or feelings that are hard to face. Skilled guides and therapists who understand this complexity are vital. They help participants hold the discomfort safely, preventing retraumatization and allowing awe to open protective but healing doors.

Lastly, not all psychedelic experiences produce awe immediately, and that can be frustrating. Sometimes the medicine needs time, repeated sessions, or different settings to open that sensation. Patience is key. As Paul Stamets reminds us, the mushroom medicine is a teacher, not a magic bullet. Respecting the process means giving yourself room to grow into awe rather than forcing it.

In my own practice, I encourage people to journal difficult moments and revisit them with curiosity later. Sometimes awe arrives after the fact, in reflection or dreams. It’s a gradual unfolding, not an instant download. Expecting this gradual process helps reduce resistance and fear.

So, while awe is beautiful and powerful, it’s not always easy or simple. Facing its shadows and challenges head-on with support can turn those moments into foundational breakthroughs.

6. Integration with daily life - long-term perspective

After a psychedelic session, the brain is like wet cement - malleable, open, but ready to harden again. Awe experienced during the trip can spark long-term shifts in perspective, but only if nurtured. Integration is the slow work of taking that awe out of the session space and into daily life.

One way to do this is by incorporating regular awe practices. These don’t require mushrooms. Simple acts like watching a sunrise, listening to inspiring music, or practicing gratitude can expand your sense of wonder and connectedness. As Dacher Keltner’s research shows, awe promotes prosocial behavior, humility, and better mental health, all valuable qualities to cultivate outside of the psychedelic experience.

Incorporating neurogenesis-supportive supplements such as Lion’s Mane mushrooms can also help maintain brain flexibility over time. Lion’s Mane supports nerve growth factor (NGF) production, aiding the brain’s ability to form new connections. This complements the rewiring catalyzed by psilocybin. If you haven’t tried it, you can find reliable options like Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules.

Mindfulness and meditation train the brain to rest more easily in the open, receptive states that psychedelics temporarily create. Many people report that these practices make them more likely to access awe spontaneously, without chemicals. It’s a way of “remembering” the psychedelic state in daily consciousness.

Socially, sharing your awe experiences can deepen their impact. Talking openly with supportive friends or participating in community groups centered on psychedelic integration can reinforce these expanded perspectives. Shame and isolation often diminish the protective value of awe, while connection strengthens it.

From a psychological angle, long-term integration requires acknowledging the challenges that come with awe, including vulnerability and uncertainty. But leaning into these feelings can build resilience and deepen your relationship with existence.

Ultimately, awe can become a compass for living. When your brain remembers how vast and interconnected life is, your choices, relationships, and values shift accordingly. The daily grind feels less heavy when viewed through this lens. That’s the quiet medicine of awe, working gently but powerfully over time.

7. Closing reflection - NOT a summary, a new thought to sit with

Awe is a strange visitor in our modern lives. It slips through cracks in our routines, catches us unawares, and leaves us simultaneously smaller and larger than ourselves. Psychedelics, and mushrooms in particular, offer a direct invitation to meet awe face-to-face on their own terms - raw, wild, and unfiltered.

But here’s a thought I’ve carried with me for years: awe doesn’t just happen to us. It happens within us. It’s not only about the external vastness of mountains, stars, or psychedelic visions. It’s about the vastness inside - the deep wells of consciousness, memory, and possibility that we often forget exist.

Every time we sit with a mushroom ceremony, every time we wander beneath the night sky, we’re touching that inner vastness. The brain’s shifting circuits, the serotonin pulses, the quieting ego - they’re not merely chemical tricks. They’re doorways into the deep mystery of being alive.

So my invitation isn’t just to seek awe as an experience but to learn from it - to carry its humility, wonder, and openness into the hard edges of daily life. To let the medicine remind you that you are part of something bigger, yes, but also that the bigger is part of you. That circle, that dance, is the heart of what psychedelics show us.

With time, patience, and attention, awe can become more than a fleeting episode. It can become a way of seeing, living, and healing. And perhaps that is the quiet medicine we all need most.
